sentences.text: Yes, to across the bridge.
And we-- we lived with these people for a few weeks.
These people-- it so happened that we had it very bad with them.
Because those were two sisters who were never married, and they had a brother who was mentally sick.
And they were giving us the worst trouble that you could encounter.
These were Jewish people.
But, you know, they were sick people.
You can't help it.
And-- but that didn't last long.
We did the best that we could.
We weren't able to do any work at that time.
We just existed.
We weren't even able to take everything with us, because there was no place to put it.
And--
The seven-- the seven of us lived in one room.
We-- we-- it was just like a-- a very, very poor living accommodation.
And we weren't able to work because, all of a sudden, the town became very crowded.
People came from one part of town into the other part of town, which was not equipped to handle the influx of all these people.
But we still did the best that we could.
You know, we always thought where there's life, there is hope.
But somehow, in another few weeks-- which was beginning of April, shortly before Passover of 1941-- they notified us by way of-- they didn't really deal with the Jews individually.
They had the Jewish committee notified that-- they gave us, I think, three days to pack our belongings and-- and leave.
And there was-- there was a destination, Bedzin.
Did you have-- yeah.
They notified us by word of mouth, by, like, messengers.
There was-- nobody had a telephone.
And they sent out messengers to notify us.
They gave us three days in which to pack whatever we could and go to the city to-- to-- to meet at the train station.
And from there, we were going to the town of the Bedzin.
Well, we packed whatever we could.
We took some furniture, also in pieces.
And we gathered at the rail station, and they packed us into the train.
Of course overloaded.
It was filled to capacity.
And then we came into Bedzin, which was--
I already don't recall.
It wasn't too long.
It was a couple of hours, maybe four hours, because it wasn't too far away.
And after we arrived in Bedzin, which was a few kilometers away from the town of Sosnowiec, where my father's sister had lived.
So my father went by himself.
He went to see his sister.
And, of course, they welcomed us in their house for temporary living.
And then, the town of Sosnowiec was six kilometers away from Myslowice, where we lived before the war.
And so my father was able to-- after staying at his sister's house-- we were able to-- we were not able to return to the Sosnowiec.
I beg your pardon-- to Myslowice.
But next to Myslowice was the little town of Modrzejow where we used to meet my father across the bridge.
And there, we knew some people who arranged for accommodation for us to come and live in that little town of Modrzejow.
So we went there.
For a while.
Maybe two weeks.
From there, we moved to Modrzejow in 194-- yeah, April 1941.
And there we were living in reasonable freedom.
Then we already had to wear, instead of the white armband with the blue star, we had to wear a yellow star on-- on our chests.
And, of course, we had a curfew.
But we lived in reasonable freedom.
No.
No school.
No school.
Rations.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Official-- Officially we weren't able to do any work, like in offices or things like that.
But whoever had a trade, like my father did, or other people did, they were able to pursue their trade, and work, and make some money, and live.
Well, we used to meet kids in-- in houses.
We were not able to gather in the street, because more than three Jews at one time in the street was too dangerous, even young people.
Because the-- they-- they already had-- beside the SS, they already had the Schutzpolizei, which they called Shupo.
They already incorporated this part as Germany.
As opposed to other parts of Poland, which were called-- excuse me-- Occupied Poland, this was already Germany.
So they already employed their own police, beside the SS, which took care of the Jewish interests.
And we had the Jewish committee, of course.
And whatever-- whatever pertained to Jews, all the edicts were notified to the Jewish committee, and they notified us in turn.
In people's homes.
I-- you know, come to think about it, we really had a good time.
You know, it's-- it's-- it's-- it's a funny thing that, when you remember these things, that as long as they let us live, we were alive, and doing what we thought was normal for the time being.
The shul was-- people did go to shul.
People did go to shul.
No, no.
That was a small shul that people went to.
And in fact, I remember even Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, my father went to shul.
And that was 1941.
He still was able to go to shul.
And it-- everything was done in a very limited way.
I-- I really don't know why they gave us that much freedom at that time.
I don't suppose that they knew what to do with us, you know, come to think about it.
And so that continued.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was supposedly normal life at the time.
Then what happened?
In May of 1942, by that time, we already knew that people were being sent to concentration camp.
We heard of this one and that one being-- being sent away.
And we already knew of names of camps.
Actually, these were forced labor camps.
That's what they were called-- Arbeitslager.
And we knew about people mailing postcards and letters, and receiving mail from home.
And we thought that, since we were, like, young, maybe we wouldn't be taken away.
Or maybe myself-- which I was already getting to be 18, 19 years old-- I thought that maybe they would take us.
But that time, they were taking mostly young men and sending them away to-- to work.
No, we didn't know what the camps were about.
They were not extermination camps.
We knew that for sure because people were mailing letters.
And they-- they used to write, like, in code.
Because we all thought that the letters were being censored.
I remember people would send, I am so happy to be here.
My every day is like a holiday, Yom Kippur.
Which, of course, is a fast day.
And so naturally, they must have received food-- you can't live without food-- but very little.
And they worked, but being young, I suppose it wasn't too hard on them.
And that was going on until 1942.
One May night, we heard knocking on the door, and aufstehen, meaning get up.
It must have been maybe 6:00 in the morning, and we all got up and opened the door.
And the SS came in, get dressed and come with us.
So we got dressed, and we all-- not knowing what's going on, we all grabbed something extra.
And they brought us to a meadow, where they gathered the entire town.
All the Jews from town were gathered on that meadow, next to the river.
And there they kept us for hours and hours and hours.
Later on, they separated old people from young people.
Myself, my sister, and my brother were all taken away.
My parents and my two younger chil-- my two younger brother and sister, we didn't know that, because we were all separated and taken away.
At that time, we didn't know that they were permitted to go home.
Later on, we found out that that was the case.
Several old people were taken away, and sent direct to Auschwitz.
And not until-- they took us to the town of Sosnowiec, where there was a gathering point.
A gathering buil-- there was a building on a street called Skladova, and there they kept us.
At first, they separated the boys from the girls.
My brother was separated from us.
My sister and I stayed together.
And we stayed there-- I don't remember exactly if it's 10 days or two weeks.
And my parents found out where we were.
They were able to come and bring us some food.
And then we were sent out.
We didn't know where we were going until we got there, of course.
We were sent to a place called Parschnitz in-- in Czechoslovakia.
In--
No.
Never again.
No.
No.
Because my brother was sent to a male camp.
Actually, my brother was sent to Markstat, and we never saw our parents again.
That time, we were sent to that place in Parschnitz, to a camp which was housed in an old factory building.
And the owner of the fact-- the old factory building had-- the new factory was placed in a different building in a town called Trautenau, which was six kilometers away.
And we were brought to that place.
We marched 6 kilometers on foot, back and forth.
We were brought to that place, where we were assigned jobs in the factory.
That factory produced-- I wouldn't call it yarn.
Thread.
From the first process, where the raw material was being brought in.
And all us girls were employed in this factory.
And my sister and I were assigned to the same department.
And this department was, like, in chain of being-- of the material being processed, we were, like, the second [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] form of processing.
It was very dirty type of work.
The machines were was huge.
It was-- they were high up to the ceiling, and each of us was working at two machines.
We were-- the machines were being fed by that-- raw material was being thrown into the front.
They were being combed out of the-- of the dirt, and then it was being fed into that huge machine.
And it came out in ribbons, like.
They were about four inches wide, and they were-- they were coming out of the machine into barrels.
These barrels were later sent out to different departments, where they were being refined and refined and refined Until they were being put on spools and shipped somewhere else.
That was our job, working at these machines.
We were feeding the machines.
We had to watch that the threads came out even.
When the-- we had to continually work at it.
It was a continuous job, because the machines were huge.
At certain times we had to jump on top of the machine.

Actually, not on top, like-- like practically in the middle of the machine, between the front part where you feed the raw material, and the back part of the machine, where the wool goes through and through.
Sometimes a thread had to-- sometimes when the feed didn't go in evenly, then the-- it didn't-- it ripped, and we had to connect it to-- for it to work properly.
In fact, at one time, my sister almost had her fingers cut off, because the machine stopped at the right-- at just in the nick of time.
Because when the thread broke, she had to apply it, put it under the needles, and then it stopped.
She was very fortunate.
At night time, we returned back to camp.
Well, the living conditions were such that we had-- it was a lower hall and an upper hall.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were housed there, maybe 150.
That camp was not too large at the time.
When we arrived there, actually we were the second transport to arrive there.
Because the camp originated, I think, in January or February of that year, 1942.
And the first transport, I think, consisted of about 100 people.
And our transport consisted of maybe another 100 people, approximately.
I can't remember exactly.
And we slept in bunk beds.
It was a lower bunk and an upper bunk.
We had these-- of course not mattresses-- but these straw bags.
They were filled with straw.
And we were given a blanket and a straw filled pillow.
And there were-- there were two rows of bunks.
There was actually four rows.
There was front, back, front, back, and tables in the middle, with benches, on which we ate.
There was a kitchen on the same level, and they did the cooking for us.
And the food was very sparse, unsatisfactory.
We were given-- when we got up in the morning, we were given-- actually, we didn't have breakfast.
We were given a container of coffee and a-- a couple of slices of bread, and we took this along with us to-- to work.
At work, we had a half hour for lunch.
And, of course, I don't think that maybe they did it on account of us.
But the German and Czech people who worked in the factories, who were at the supervisory capacity, they had to have time for lunch.
And then we had that food that we brought along with us.
We each-- we each had-- were given a can in which to put the coffee.

And at night, when we ret-- after the day was done, after we returned to-- to the camp, we received a big spoonful of-- of food, which was-- they call it Eintopf, which meant that it's all cooked in one pot.
And there was mostly vegetables.
Sometimes there were a few pieces of meat.
Going back to the work that we did, the work was so dirty that the place was mostly-- you could feel like you were-- like you were in a fog, because it was very dusty.
All that-- that work, that was-- the machine were beating out the dust from the material, it was all-- it all came into the air.
And we were breathing in the dirt.
I remember some girls got TB, tuberculosis.
And I remember that I tried to keep my lungs clear by blowing my nose often and bringing up my saliva, which was black all the time.
And we were-- they-- we were given uniforms to wear at work, which-- which were covering everything except our hands and our faces.
We covered our faces all the way.
And most the time, we had to wipe out our eyes because our eyelashes were always full of dust, and so, of course, were our faces.
And when we came back after a day's work, we had-- we had showers.
We were able to clean up, which was good.
We were able to clean up, and that's when we-- when we got our food.
And then after-- after-- we were-- 9:00 was lights out, so we did things that we needed to do.
We had to wash our clothes and mend whatever we could.
Though we really didn't have much.
We were-- at that time we were able to keep our clothes.
No.
No.
It was-- at certain times, it was six days a week.
At certain times, it was five days a week.
It wasn't seven days.
We had Sunday.
We did have Sunday, yes.
Oh, we couldn't go anywhere, of course.
We were-- the camp was watched.
It was guarded.
And we couldn't go anywhere.
So what did we do?
As I said, we did our laundry.
We did our hair.
We relaxed a little.
In the beginning, we were able to receive mail for about, I would say, maybe a year.
We were able to receive mail and packages from home.
And we were able-- and then we wrote mail.
And we would just, on a nice day, we would go out in the yard and-- and relax.
And do like-- like any worker, except that we were-- we were imprisoned because we didn't have any freedom to go anywhere.
Well, at first, we-- as I said, at first we were able to mail cards, letters, and we were able to receive it back.
We didn't know where our brother was until, when my brother was able to communicate with our parents, that's when they notified us where he was at.
But we were not able to communicate with him, because, since we were able to communicate with the outside world, we were not able to communicate with another camp.
And that was-- and that's the way it was going on for, I would say, two years.
There was always fear.
There was always fear.
We also had, since we worked with the Czech and German people in the factory, some of them were very kind.
And they would give us a little food, which we really appreciated, of course.
And still we lived.
We lived.
You know, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the woman who was running the camp, she was called Lagerfuhrerin.
She was the camp-- the kommand-- the kommandant, the woman camp kommandant.
Her husband was like a liaison between the factory and the camp.
And he was a real spy.
He was spying on the girls.
If they got-- get too chummy with the population, the Czech and the German population, and if he saw something that he didn't like, he reported it to his wife.

In fact, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sometime-- at one time, as a matter of fact, she sent three girls to concentration camp in Auschwitz as a reprisal for being a little too chummy with the-- with the civilian people.
They were nice to her.
She was-- some people are just nice, you know, in every nationality, and this woman, at first, she-- this girl in particular, she was a daughter whose parents were old.
And at the time when we were first gathered at the river, when they first took us out of the house-- 1942, May 12-- her parents were immediately sent to Auschwitz and exterminated.
And she was-- she was an only child.
She was very bitter.
Very-- we were all bitter, of course, but she knew for a fact that her parents were exterminated.
We-- since we knew later that our parents went home, we were still hoping.
Well, that was going on for about maybe a year.
Yes, yes, they gave us paper.
And-- yes, they gave us paper.
And for that year, things were going on.
We worked, we came home, we did our thing, we went to sleep, and we went to work the next day.
And then we start-- yeah, my father still wrote cards to us.
And he didn't let on about all the things that happened.
And then mail stopped, I think, close to, like, the end of 1942, beginning of '43.
The mail stopped coming.
So then they brought in another transport of people, and then we found out that the town of Modrzejow was converted into a ghetto.
And the two younger children remained there-- the brother and the sister remained in the ghetto.
But since we didn't have any further information, we really didn't know what went on after that.

And in 19-- in the beginning of 1943-- of course, we didn't know anything that was going on in the world, because at the factory, we were being watched by not SS people, profess-- who were Germans, but they were recruited from the Volksdeutsche population.
This part of Czechoslovakia was also-- they called Sudetendeutsch.
You know?
And there was a lot of German people.
And some of these people were recruited into the SS to become our guards.
They were guarding us at the factory and at the camp.
Some of these people we knew, because previously, we worked with them.
So-- but then we were finding-- we were finding out little things, because the-- the head-- how should I say-- of the department, who was German, he lost two sons on the Russian front.
Of course, he wasn't too happy about it.
So sometimes he would go around and cry.
And he wasn't too bad of a person, so he told us.
So we were kind of happy.
Every time we heard bad news happening to the Germans, we were very happy about it.
Let's stop right there and we'll change tapes.
Yes.
Yes.
